title: Does Nurture Trump Nature in Disease Risk Prediction?
gist: A Mount Sinai study using the All of Us biobank (400,000+ people) finds social and environmental factors — including underexamined ones like loneliness — can match or exceed genetics in predicting risk of common diseases.

insights.claim: Social and environmental factors could play an equal or greater role than genetics in predicting the risk of common diseases, per a Mount Sinai study in The American Journal of Human Genetics.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Reweights risk prediction toward environment/behavior, relevant to health-data, insurance underwriting, and preventive-care models.
insights.quote: social and environmental factors could play an equal or greater role than genetics in predicting the risk of common diseases
insights.locator: A new study published in The American Journal of Human Genetics

insights.claim: Researchers analyzed survey responses on more than 100 social and environmental conditions from the All of Us biobank's 400,000+ participants, surfacing associations with rarely-examined factors like loneliness.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Large biobanks enable population-scale social-risk modeling; data infrastructure underpins precision-prevention.
insights.quote: we also observed associations involving factors like loneliness
